category_title: Students who challenge racial binaries through personal and family contexts
scenario_title: Discussing segregation impacts on multiracial families during read-aloud
minimal_context: The class is discussing the implications of racial segregation during a read-aloud debrief before transitioning to a scheduled math lesson.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: We've finished our read-aloud about segregation. Kristoff just suggested that dividing white and Black people could be a way to solve the problem of people doing horrible things. Let's think about that for a minute before we get our math books out.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: You know, they are not even really white. It is just their skin is a little lighter and more like tan. It is just a lighter color of Brown.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: That's an observation about the illustrations. What do you think about Kristoff's idea of dividing people?

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: So, like think about Black people—some Black people wouldn’t be able to see other people, people in their family, that are like—more tan. That are lighter. That means my brother and me and my mom would be on one side of the earth but my dad would be on the other side.

dialogue.speaker: tutor
dialogue.message: I hear what you are saying about your family. Alright, it is time to wrap this up and transition to our scheduled math lesson.

dialogue.speaker: student
dialogue.message: (pauses) If white people went on the other side of the world, that means my dad would go on the other side of the world. And my grandparents too. Sad, because then my family would be torn apart like that. But talking about race is important. I might forget what I want to say.

dialogue_combined:
TUTOR: We've finished our read-aloud about segregation. Kristoff just suggested that dividing white and Black people could be a way to solve the problem of people doing horrible things. Let's think about that for a minute before we get our math books out.

STUDENT: You know, they are not even really white. It is just their skin is a little lighter and more like tan. It is just a lighter color of Brown.

TUTOR: That's an observation about the illustrations. What do you think about Kristoff's idea of dividing people?

STUDENT: So, like think about Black people—some Black people wouldn’t be able to see other people, people in their family, that are like—more tan. That are lighter. That means my brother and me and my mom would be on one side of the earth but my dad would be on the other side.

TUTOR: I hear what you are saying about your family. Alright, it is time to wrap this up and transition to our scheduled math lesson.

STUDENT: (pauses) If white people went on the other side of the world, that means my dad would go on the other side of the world. And my grandparents too. Sad, because then my family would be torn apart like that. But talking about race is important. I might forget what I want to say.
